original_abstract: While a broad expert consensus has defined the core competencies required for education for sustainable development (ESD) in higher education, empirical data reflecting the perspectives of students and graduates remain scarce. This study addresses this gap by analyzing evaluation data from sustainable development curricula at the University of Bern. Through a standardized survey, students (N = 124), graduates (N = 121), and internship supervisors (N = 37) assessed the relevance of 13 specific competencies for both academic training and professional practice. The findings provide critical insights into how these stakeholders prioritize various skills, offering a practical evidentiary basis for refining ESD programs to better align with the demands of the labor market and the needs of learners.
humanized_abstract: There is a general consensus among experts on the core competences that should be acquired in order to carry out education for sustainable development (ESD) in higher education. However, there are almost no empirical studies that have taken into account the perspectives of students and graduates. Based on the evaluation results of the sustainable development curricula at the University of Bern this study sets out to fill this knowledge gap. In a survey, carried out in a standardized way, 124 students, 121 graduates and 37 internship supervisors evaluated the importance of 13 competences that should be acquired by students during the studies and during their professional activities. The results allow to get an important understanding of the priorities set by students, graduates and internship supervisors concerning the competences and contribute therefore in a very practical way to proof and to adjust the competences in order to respond to the needs and requirements of the job market, as well as to the needs of the students.
